data.title: Bayer gains after raising 2025 outlook
data.description:
Bayer (OTCPK:BAYRY) (OTCPK:BAYZF) shares traded higher in Germany on Friday after the company lifted its full-year guidance for currency-adjusted sales and earnings, citing a better-than-expected performance from its pharma division.

Announcing its preliminary Q2 2025 results, the company revised its 2025 currency-adjusted financial outlook for revenue and core earnings per share to €46B–€48B and €4.80–€5.30 from the prior forecasts of €45B–€47B and €4.50–€5.00, respectively.

The German conglomerate also added an extra €1.2B ($1.37) provision related to its ongoing Roundup (glyphosate) litigation in the U.S., mainly to account for an adverse ruling on its appeal in the Anderson et al. case.

The additional provision came after a Missouri appellate court in May upheld a $611M verdict awarded to three people who alleged that Roundup caused their cancers.

As for preliminary Q2 results, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) reported €1.23 of core earnings per share on group sales of ~€10.7, driven by 2.2% YoY growth in its Crop Science Division, while its Pharmaceuticals and Consumer Health divisions expanded 0.6% and 0.2%, respectively, on an adjusted basis.

data.title: U.S. Manufacturing PMI contracts less than expected in July
data.description: The S&P Global U.S. Manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index fell to 49.8 in July from 52.9 in June, compared with the 49.5 consensus.

data.title: Cognex gets rating upgrade at KeyBanc after Q2 results, outlook
data.description:
KeyBanc Capital upgraded Cognex's (NASDAQ:CGNX) stock to Overweight from Sector Weight with a $50 price target.

Analysts led by Ken Newman said that following Cognex's second quarter 2025 results and subsequent earnings call, they upgraded the shares. Shares strongly outperformed after posting a modest second quarter beat and stronger-than-expected third quarter guidance ranges for both revenue and EBITDA.

Cognex's surged and closed about 20.76% on Thursday. However, the shares dipped about 2% on Friday.

The analysts said that specifically, the updated guidance implies an EBITDA flow-through margin of about 62% at the midpoint. The analysts think improving demand across most of the company's served end markets (i.e.. Warehouse/eCommerce, Packaging, and Factory Automation) could support a shift toward more normalized upcycle operating leverage into 2026 (with EBITDA incrementals averaging 55%+ since 2016).

Relative to KeyBanc's model, that implies out-year EBITDA margins in the low- to mid-20% range, well ahead of its 20% 2026 target introduced at its recent analyst day, and on track to meet its intermediate margin target of nearly over 25%, according to the analysts.

"While we acknowledge that macro visibility remains tepid, we think CGNX’s multiple likely already discounts that uncertainty, and think recently completed trade deals with Malaysia and Vietnam could allow some breathing room toward returning to more historical averages in the NT [near-term]," said Newman and his team.

data.title: Ares Management stock gaps down after Q2 earnings fall short of consensus
data.description:
Ares Management (NYSE:ARES) stock retreated 4.6% in Friday morning trading after the alternative investment manager second-quarter earnings that fell short of the average analyst estimate, despite strong gross fundings and market gains buoying assets under management.

Q2 adjusted GAAP EPS of $1.03, trailing the $1.09 average analyst estimate, slid from $1.09 in the prior quarter and rose from $0.99 in Q2 2024.

"Mixed contributions from fee-related earnings and realized performance income" drove the earnings miss, CFRA analyst Kenneth Leon wrote in a note to clients.

Even so, “we generated strong second quarter results, growing AUM, fee paying AUM and management fees by 24% or more year over year,” said CEO Michael Arougheti. “Our AUM benefited from our second highest level of quarterly gross fundraising coupled with record quarterly market appreciation driven by strong investment performance across our major strategies.

Fee-related earnings advanced to $397.8M in Q2 from $367.3M in Q1 and $324.5M in the year-ago period.

Realized income was $397.8M, down from $405.9M in Q1 and up from $363.2M a year before.

Total expenses of $1.14B compared with $1.01B in Q1 and $564.5M in Q2 2024.

“Our second quarter includes the first full quarter of financials from the GCP International acquisition, and we believe we are on track to meet our financial goals for this business, including greater than originally expected synergies that will be gradually realized over time,” said CFO Jarrod Phillips.

Gross new capital commitments totaled $26.2B in Q2, up from $20.2B in the prior three-month period. 

"We like that ARES has $151B of dry powder for future investment and $105B of AUM not yet paying fees within its funds," CFRA's Leon said.

Assets under management rose to $572.4B at June 30, 2025, from $545.9B at March 31, 2025, and climbed 28% from a year ago.

Conference call at 11:00 a.m. ET.

data.title: Piper Sandler signals robust advisory pipeline and G Squared acquisition as momentum builds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Piper Sandler Companies (PIPR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Chad R. Abraham described the quarter as one that began with uncertainty and volatility, but by mid-May, "market sentiment shifted, equity markets recovered and confidence improved." He highlighted that the company closed the quarter with adjusted net revenues of $405 million, an 18.1% operating margin, and adjusted EPS of $2.95, all higher than the previous year. Abraham emphasized, "Advisory revenues were $206 million during the quarter, up 12% year-over-year," with the Services and Industrials group delivering one of their best quarters since 2021, attributed to the "continued addition of high-quality talent that focuses on sub verticals, important to our clients." He noted strategic expansion in industry and product capabilities, particularly in debt advisory, which "has been very active, and we continue to experience strong demand for their services."
  • Abraham reported that during the first half of 2025, advisory revenues reached $423 million, up 24% year-over-year, driven by both M&A and non-M&A revenues, including debt advisory, private capital advisory, and restructuring. He stated, "Diversification from both a sector and product perspective, benefited us during the first half of 2025 even as the number of completed middle market M&A transactions declined year-over-year."
  • On the corporate financing front, Abraham said revenues were $35 million, down 31% year-over-year, with 26 financings completed totaling $10 billion raised. He noted ongoing sector-specific challenges, particularly a "61% decline in the economic fee pool for biopharma companies."
  • Abraham also announced the acquisition of G Squared Capital Partners, expected to close in Q3 2025, stating, "The pending acquisition will further the growth of our technology investment banking group by combining their deep government sector experience with our cybersecurity and broader technology expertise."
  • President Debbra Lynn Schoneman highlighted a strong public finance business: "For the second quarter of 2025, we generated $42 million of municipal financing revenues, up 66% year-over-year, exceeding the market issuance growth in par value of 15%." She also reported equity brokerage revenues of $58 million, up 12% year-over-year, and fixed income revenues of $54 million, up 21% from Q1 and 37% from last year.
  • CFO Katherine Patricia Clune stated, "We generated net revenues of $405 million for the second quarter of 2025. Operating income was $73 million, resulting in an operating margin of 18.1%. We delivered $53 million of net income and $2.95 of diluted EPS." Clune also reported a $5 million restructuring charge related to headcount reductions and vacated office space from the Aviditi Advisors acquisition.
  • Clune concluded, "Effective today, the Board approved a $0.05 increase to our quarterly cash dividend to $0.70 per share."

Outlook

  • Abraham shared, "We have a robust pipeline of announced and in-process transactions, and we expect our third quarter advisory revenues to be largely consistent with the second quarter."
  • Schoneman indicated moderation in public finance and brokerage revenues: "We have a robust pipeline, yet expect third quarter revenues to moderate from the very strong second quarter," and "We expect revenues to moderate from second quarter levels as volatility has normalized."
  • Clune said, "We'd expect for the remainder of the year to be in this [compensation ratio] range without providing any sort of specific figure... barring any significant outsized investments or kind of turn in the market conditions."

Financial Results

  • Net revenues for Q2 2025 totaled $405 million, an increase of 6% from Q1 and 14% over the prior year period. Operating margin was 18.1%. Net income was $53 million, and diluted EPS was $2.95.
  • Advisory services accounted for 54% of total net revenues for the first half, increasing 24% year-over-year. Municipal financing and equity brokerage businesses delivered record revenues for the first half.
  • Compensation ratio was 62% for Q2. Non-compensation expenses, excluding reimbursed deal costs, were $69 million, up 6% year-over-year, driven by higher legal and professional fees.
  • Capital return included 85,000 shares repurchased for $21 million and $17 million paid in dividends for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Devin Patrick Ryan, Citizens: Asked about the impact of improved bank M&A conditions on revenue timing. Abraham responded, "We are definitely seeing proof of regulatory approvals being quicker... I do think some of these will -- some of the stuff because it's closing faster will close later in the year, but I still think we'll feel a lot of this impact next year."
  • Ryan followed up on Aviditi integration. Abraham stated, "That transaction has worked out sort of exactly as we had hoped... the results have been pretty good. So we're very pleased with how that's going."
  • James Edwin Yaro, Goldman Sachs: Queried the IPO and biotech outlook. Abraham said, "...the IPO market is improving. In general, we were off in July here. We've done some nice transactions in biotech, but it's coming off of a slow pace."
  • Yaro also asked about fixed income trading and bank M&A. Schoneman explained, "It's a little more of just having a really strong Q2 than seeing anything that might seem inconsistent... we did have a very strong -- a couple of strong -- very large transactions in the second quarter."
  • Brendan James O'Brien, Wolfe Research: Asked about sponsor environment and progress toward the $2 billion investment banking target. Abraham replied, "I think we have even more conviction as time goes by relative to each industry team where we're going to see the growth, where we're going to add the MDs, and we still believe we'll see some increased productivity."
  • Michael John Grondahl, Northland Securities: Inquired about healthcare advisory outlook. Abraham confirmed, "Healthcare was our only team that was down last year. It's been quite strong this year, probably our team that's up the most in advisory."
  • Grondahl probed further on debt advisory and private capital market transactions, with Abraham detailing the growth and variety of transactions in these areas.
  • Yaro asked about compensation and expense trajectory. Clune confirmed, "We'd expect for the remainder of the year to be in this range... barring any significant outsized investments or kind of turn in the market conditions."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on the durability of improvements in M&A and bank consolidation, sought clarity on expense trends, and questioned the pace of recovery in IPO and sponsor activity, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with a focus on forward momentum and risk factors.
  • Management exhibited confidence, especially in advisory growth, the Aviditi integration, and the G Squared acquisition, using phrases such as "we are well positioned to gain share" and "we have even more conviction as time goes by." During Q&A, responses remained measured, occasionally hedging timelines for recovery with statements like "I still think we'll feel a lot of this impact next year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts were less focused on near-term volatility and more on the sustainability of improving trends, while management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to measured confidence, especially regarding advisory, public finance, and technology-focused expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Compared to Q1 2025, Q2 saw improved market sentiment and higher net revenues, operating margin, and EPS.
  • Management guidance shifted from caution and uncertainty to a more constructive outlook, with a robust deal pipeline and expectations for stable advisory revenues in Q3.
  • Strategic focus broadened with the G Squared acquisition, furthering technology and government sector capabilities, whereas the prior quarter highlighted cautious pipeline conversion amid volatility.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused on the trajectory of M&A, productivity improvements, and the integration of recent acquisitions, whereas Q1 questions centered on volatility, sector-specific risks, and near-term deal delays.
  • Expense management remained a recurring topic, with updated commentary on compensation and non-compensation expense trends in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged a $5 million restructuring charge due to headcount reductions and vacated office space from the Aviditi Advisors acquisition.
  • Sector-specific challenges persist, particularly in biopharma, where the economic fee pool declined 61% year-over-year.
  • Fixed income revenues are anticipated to soften in Q3 following a strong Q2, with Schoneman noting, "we anticipate fixed income revenues to soften in the third quarter."
  • Analysts expressed concern about the pace of recovery in IPOs, bank M&A, and the sponsor environment, as well as the trajectory of compensation and non-compensation expenses.
  • Management cited the need for ongoing operating discipline and careful talent management in response to market and business shifts.

Final Takeaway

Piper Sandler delivered strong Q2 results with improved advisory, municipal financing, and institutional brokerage revenues, supported by diversification and talent additions. The company expects continued momentum in advisory revenues for Q3, while acknowledging moderation in other segments. The acquisition of G Squared Capital Partners underscores a strategic push into technology and government sectors. Expense management and talent optimization remain priorities as the company navigates sector-specific headwinds and prepares for further market share gains in the back half of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SA Sentiment: Will the Fed only cut interest rates once in 2025?
data.description:
As the Federal Reserve left its benchmark interest rate unchanged once again at 4.25%-4.50%, market participants are anticipating no more than two cuts this year.

Fed policymakers gave no indication of the direction of its next rate move. To note, two board governors voted to cut rates by 25 basis points, the first double dissent since 1993. 

Markets had been pricing in another pause at the Fed's meeting in September, according to the CME FedWatch Tool, but those expectations have changed since the release of July's non-farm payrolls data on Friday.

Jobs growth in July was well below consensus estimates, and data from the last two months saw massive revisions, indicating that job creation was much weaker than expected.  

At the time of publishing, the odds of the Fed bringing its policy rate to 3.75%-4.00% by the end of the year increased significantly.

Turning to Seeking Alpha readers, expectations once again mounted for one cut this year. According to July's Sentiment Survey, which gathered around 1,000 responses, the percentage of respondents expecting one cut increased by 8 percentage points M/M and up 33 pp since January.

The percentage of respondents expecting two cuts decreased by 21 pp since the start of the year. To note, the survey was taken before the Fed's policy decision and the jobs report this week.

Here's a look at SA readers' rate cut expectations so far this year:

data.title: Euronet reaffirms 12%-16% earnings growth target for 2025 as CoreCard acquisition accelerates digital expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Euronet Worldwide (EEFT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Michael J. Brown, Chairman, CEO & President, opened by announcing the acquisition of CoreCard, describing it as a move that “extends our strategy into the digital payments processing space,” and emphasizing it provides "yet more growth opportunities as it enables us to go after the $10 billion-plus revenue TAM market with very attractive operating margins approaching 50%." Brown highlighted a newly signed Ren deal with one of the three largest U.S. banks, noting “our Ren technology will be used to drive thousands of ATMs across the country,” and underscored that this win demonstrates the “capability and confidence that banking leaders have in our Ren platform.”
  • Brown reported, “The second quarter, we delivered constant currency operating income growth year-over-year of 13%.”
  • Rick L. Weller, CFO, stated, “We delivered a record second quarter on all key reported consolidated metrics. We delivered revenue of $1.1 billion, operating income of $159 million and adjusted EBITDA of $206 million; and finally, adjusted EPS of $2.56.” Weller added, “Money Transfer segment led the way by producing constant currency operating income growth of 33% despite significant macro uncertainties.”
  • Weller noted, “We repurchased $247 million of our shares. Given the timing of the repurchases, there was only a marginal benefit to the second quarter adjusted EPS.”
  • Weller highlighted, “Our consolidated operating margins expanded by more than 112 basis points over the prior year, and we expect to see a continuation of posting expanded margins as we go through the second half of the year.”
  • Brown described the strategic rationale for CoreCard: “This acquisition marks a strategic milestone in our long-term growth plan, reinforcing our commitment to scalable, high-margin digital businesses that align with global payment trends.”

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed guidance, stating, “we are pleased to reaffirm the 12% to 16% earnings growth expectation we have for 2025.”
  • Weller indicated, “Looking forward for the balance of the year, we would expect the tax rate to tick up 1% or 2%.”
  • Brown said, “We are moving in a strong strategic digital direction as evidenced with the CoreCard acquisition and the recently announced Ren deals.”

Financial Results

  • Weller reported, “We delivered revenue of $1.1 billion, operating income of $159 million and adjusted EBITDA of $206 million; and finally, adjusted EPS of $2.56.”
  • The Money Transfer segment delivered 33% operating income growth year-over-year, with “digital transactions grew by 29%, reflecting continued consumer demand for digital products.”
  • Weller noted, “Our consolidated operating margins expanded by more than 112 basis points over the prior year.”
  • Direct-to-consumer digital transactions in Money Transfer “grew by 29%,” and “our digital payout product... is up 20% year-over-year, now composing 55% of total volume.”
  • Euronet ended the second quarter with $1.3 billion in unrestricted cash and debt of $2.4 billion.
  • Weller noted a $0.05 per share impact from higher interest expense due to carrying the refinance convertible at revolver rates and another $0.05 per share from higher income taxes.

Q&A

  • Vasundhara Govil, KBW: Asked about CoreCard's concentration risk related to Apple and confidence in accretion if that relationship ended. Brown replied that they have "these potentials... out there doing debit card issuing for a whole bunch of banks and fintechs," and factored in the risk of Apple leaving in their analysis, expecting to have Apple as a customer for "a couple of 3 years anyway."
  • Govil asked about deceleration in EFT segment growth. Brown responded, “a last year good quarter kind of hunt you this year,” attributing it to a tough comparison and expecting Q3 to see growth restoration.
  • Peter James Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: Inquired about revenue timing from the top 3 U.S. bank Ren deal. Brown said, “we already have revenue coming from them, and it's only going to accelerate... most of it is going to come fourth quarter and beyond.”
  • Heckmann asked about the $250 million software revenue target. Brown replied, “$250 million is a little steep for 2028,” and suggested more detail could come at a potential Investor Day.
  • Gustavo Andre Gala, Monness, Crespi, Hardt: Asked about the opportunity to improve CoreCard margins. Brown indicated cost synergies and volume growth would support margin improvement.
  • Gala asked about Money Transfer trends in July. Brown responded, “a big uptick over June. So we do not see any troughing. In fact, we see just the opposite. We're excited about both the digital growth... and we're also seeing retail growth.”
  • Michael John Grondahl, Northland: Asked about epay promotions and FX impact on Money Transfer. Brown and Weller said promotions were typical and FX fluctuations provided some incremental margin benefit.
  • Cristopher David Kennedy, William Blair: Inquired about CoreCard’s sustainable growth. Brown expects to "supercharge" growth by expanding internationally, leveraging Euronet’s global relationships.
  • Charles Joseph Nabhan, Stephens: Asked about the moat in revolving credit issuance. Brown explained the complexity and flexibility required, which “created this excellent product.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on risk concentration (Apple/CoreCard), growth sustainability, and margin dynamics, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone, with pressing but constructive questions.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing growth, strategic wins, and opportunity, but acknowledged risks and provided detailed mitigation strategies. Brown showed assurance, stating, “we are just crushing our numbers because we intend to be #1 in the world.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a generally positive outlook, but current questions reflected heightened scrutiny of strategic execution and risk management tied to the CoreCard acquisition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remained unchanged at 12%-16% earnings growth for 2025.
  • The major strategic shift was the announcement of the CoreCard acquisition and a significant Ren deal with a top 3 U.S. bank, compared to last quarter’s focus on continued organic growth and digital expansion.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, operating income, and digital Money Transfer growth remained strong, with Money Transfer operating income growth accelerating from 23% last quarter to 33% this quarter.
  • Analysts’ questions in both quarters were growth-focused, though this quarter showed more attention to risk concentration and international expansion.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, with an added emphasis on the transformative potential of CoreCard and Ren.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified exposure to a new 1% remittance tax, but noted this affects only 27% of Money Transfer segment or 12% of consolidated revenues, and research suggests “it will not have a significant impact on our business when it begins next January.”
  • Brown acknowledged potential revenue risk if Apple were to leave CoreCard, but explained this was factored into acquisition analysis and cross-sell opportunities are expected to offset.
  • Weller mentioned a $0.05 per share impact each from higher interest expense and tax, with expectations for the tax rate to increase by 1%-2% for the balance of the year.
  • Analysts highlighted risk from revenue concentration and asked for clarity on the timing and magnitude of software growth targets, indicating concern over execution.

Final Takeaway

Euronet’s second quarter centered on the strategic acquisition of CoreCard and a landmark Ren deal with a top 3 U.S. bank, supporting its digital transformation and reinforcing guidance for 12%-16% earnings growth in 2025. Management underscored strong momentum in Money Transfer, operating leverage, and global expansion opportunities, while addressing risk factors related to new taxes and client concentration. The company’s focus on high-margin, scalable digital solutions and cross-segment synergies positions it for continued growth, with recent moves setting the foundation for further international expansion and digital revenue acceleration.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Canada's manufacturing PMI rises to 46.10 points in July
data.description:
• Manufacturing PMI in Canada increased to 46.10 points in July from 45.60 points in June of 2025.
  • Tariffs again served to raise input costs and resulted in further supply chain delays.
  • However, in a relatively positive development, input price inflation softened to an eight-month low. In response, selling charges were raised to a reduced degree. 
  • Slower falls in both output and new orders were registered in July, though the rates of decline remained marked and extended the respective downturns to six months.

 

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWC), (NYSEARCA:FLCA).

Currency: (USD:CAD) (USD:CAD)

data.title: Nasdaq, Dow, S&P 500 fall more than 1% as jobs report comes in lower than expected
data.description:
Wall Street opened in the red on Friday, and yields tumbled as the jobs data came in cooler than expected, and traders also assessed President Donald Trump's revised tariff orders.

S&P 500 (SP500) -1%, the Dow (DJI) -1.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -1.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 12 basis points to 4.26%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 20 basis points to 3.76%.

Nonfarm payrolls came in lower than expected in July and the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.2%

Trump on Thursday signed an executive order revising “reciprocal” tariffs on dozens of countries, setting new duty rates between 10% and 41%. According to the White House, any goods deemed to be transshipped to evade these duties will incur an additional 40% tariff.

Wall Street on Thursday limped to a negative finish as a post-earnings surge in tech titans Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) was overshadowed by jitters over tariffs and economic data that showed stubborn inflation and weak personal spending.

"Equity markets have seen contrasting themes over the last 24 hours, as a resurgence of U.S. 'tech-ceptionalism' was offset by a broader loss of momentum,” Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

On the earnings side, Amazon (AMZN) was down 8% in premarket trade as its Q3 outlook disappointed. Apple (AAPL) was up 1.5% as it beat its third-quarter estimates, helped by higher-than-expected iPhone sales.

Other economic releases for the day include the July PMI manufacturing final and the July ISM manufacturing index. Both are expected to come in at 49.5.

The July consumer sentiment report will land during market hours.

data.title: LENZ Therapeutics gets FDA nod for presbyopia eye drop
data.description:
• LENZ Therapeutics (NASDAQ:LENZ) said that the U.S. FDA approved its eye drop, Vizz (aceclidine ophthalmic solution) 1.44%, for the treatment of presbyopia in adults.
  • The company stated that it expects the commercial product to be widely accessible by mid-Q4 2025, with samples expected in the United States as early as October 2025.
  • Direct-to-eye care professional sales and marketing activities to be initiated immediately, LENZ added.
  • The stock is up about 6% premarket.
